front: Poem: opens 'I do not know what it means / That I should feel so sad'; describes a golden-haired maiden on the Rhine who lures sailors to their deaths
Poem: during the Nazi period, this poem was officially declared written by an 'unknown author'
Poem: gold is a recurring motif, shared by the siren's jewelry, hair, and comb; the legend first appeared in Clemens Brentano's lay 'Zu Bacharach am Rheine'
Poem: contains sections 'Lyrisches Intermezzo,' 'Youthful Sorrows,' 'Die Heimkehr,' and 'Die Nordsee'; a seminal poetry collection frequently set to music as Lieder
Poem: its Lyrisches Intermezzo was set to music by Robert Schumann in the song cycle Dichterliebe
Poem: ends with 'Die Nordsee' (The North Sea), two cycles that mock Greek gods in 'The Song of the Oceanides' and end with 'In Port'

Poem: a satirical poem about a journey from Paris to Hamburg; the speaker relives Hermann's victory at the Battle of Teutoburg Forest and imagines explaining politics to a rewoken Frederick Barbarossa
Poem: mocks the construction of Koln Cathedral and describes being stalked by an axe-carrying 'special devil'
Poem: a 27-canto mock epic about a dancing bear pursued through the Pyrenees by the witch Uraka and her son Laskaro; subtitled 'A Midsummer Night's Dream'
Play: source of the prophetic quote 'Where they burn books, they will ultimately burn people'; set during the Spanish Inquisition in Granada
Poem: a soldier wants to be buried in full uniform to rise from the grave and defend Napoleon; one of the title soldiers says his wife and child should 'go beg'
Poem: the speaker satirically prays to keep the addressee 'so comely and beautiful and pure'; begins 'Du bist wie eine Blume'
back: Du bist wie eine Blume (Heinrich Heine)

front: Poem: concludes 'Sleep is good, death is better -- surely the best would be never to have been born'
Poem: asks 'Shall I lie in desert reaches, / Buried by a stranger's hand?'; a one-word-titled poem engraved on three sides of the poet's tomb in Paris
Poem: begins 'Their gloom-enveloped eyes are tearless'; about workers in Silesia
Author: Karl Kraus blamed this poet for infecting German literature with the 'feuilleton' style after his 1831 flight from Germany to Paris
Author: used the term 'mattress-grave' for the bed he was confined to for the last eight years of his life due to a mysterious paralytic illness
Author: coined the word 'Lisztomania'
Author: Hannah Arendt praised this poet's integration of Yiddish into German literature, identifying him as the beginning of the 'hidden tradition' of the schlemiel
Author: Travel Pictures opens with a walking tour through the Harz mountains
Author: wrote an anti-censorship text whose only words are 'censors' and 'idiots' (from the 'Ideen' section)
